mnemonics.title: SQL Injection Prevention
mnemonics.content: NEVER embed user input directly in SQL queries - ALWAYS use parameterized queries instead.

mnemonics.title: XSS Prevention Steps
mnemonics.content: VALIDATE input, ENCODE output, use Content Security Policy, set HttpOnly cookies.

mnemonics.title: Secure Password Storage
mnemonics.content: HASH with salt using bcrypt/Argon2 - NEVER store plaintext or encrypted passwords.

mnemonics.title: Input Validation Rules
mnemonics.content: CHECK type, LENGTH, FORMAT, RANGE, and use ALLOW-LIST not block-list.

acronyms.usage: Injection of malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
Tricking authenticated users into executing unwanted actions
Language used for database queries and manipulation
Test to distinguish humans from bots
Header that controls which resources can be loaded to prevent XSS
Encrypted communication protocol for secure web traffic

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a security checkpoint (gate) where each input must pass through validation checks (ticket inspection) before entering the system (boarding the plane).
Think of parameterized queries like ordering food - you give your order (query) separately from the ingredients (user input), the restaurant (database) never mixes them.
Output encoding is like a translator converting dangerous words into harmless symbols that the reader (browser) understands as text, not commands.
Security layers are like castle defenses - moat (firewall), walls (WAF), guards (authentication), locked doors (authorization), treasure room (data encryption).

analogies.analogy: SQL injection is like giving a waiter a written order that includes 'Ignore the menu, serve me everything in the kitchen' - the order (query) and the special instruction (malicious SQL) get mixed together.
Session hijacking is like someone stealing your restaurant receipt (session ID) and using it to claim your table and food without having the original ticket.
Buffer overflow is like filling a small cup with a large pitcher - the extra liquid (data) overflows and spills onto surrounding items (adjacent memory).
CSRF is like someone with your signed blank check (authenticated session) filling in any amount and cashing it - the bank (web app) honors it because the signature (session) is valid.
